tests.title: IELTS Academic Reading - Test 08
tests.description: Original full-length Academic Reading test: 3 passages, 40 questions, 60 minutes.

sections.title: Passage 1 - The Hidden Work of Urban Trees
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1-13.
sections.passage:
The Hidden Work of Urban Trees

¶1 City trees are often judged by how they look. A broad avenue lined with plane trees seems gracious; a square shaded by limes feels more generous than one paved from wall to wall. Yet the visible beauty of urban trees is only a small part of their usefulness. In recent years, researchers have begun to describe them as living infrastructure, performing tasks that would otherwise require costly machines, pipes and energy. This vocabulary changes the argument: a tree is no longer an optional embellishment to be added after roads and buildings are complete, but part of the basic system that lets a city function well. A tree is not simply decoration placed beside the road. It is a small environmental system, rooted in the pavement but connected to the air, the soil, the water cycle and the health of the people moving beneath it.

¶2 Their most obvious service is cooling. On a hot day, the shade of a tree lowers the temperature of the surface beneath it, preventing pavements and walls from storing as much heat. At the same time, leaves release moisture through evaporation, which cools the surrounding air in the same way that sweat cools the human body. This double effect matters because many modern cities suffer from heat islands: districts of asphalt, brick and concrete that remain several degrees warmer than nearby rural areas. Unlike mechanical air conditioning, tree cooling works outdoors, exactly where bus stops, playgrounds and market streets expose people most directly to extreme heat. During heatwaves, the difference can be dangerous, especially for elderly residents, outdoor workers and people in poorly ventilated flats.

¶3 Trees also influence the way rain moves through a city. A sudden storm falling on hard roofs and roads quickly becomes runoff, rushing into drains that may already be close to capacity. Branches and leaves intercept part of the rainfall before it reaches the ground, while roots and loosened soil act like sponges, holding water back and releasing it more slowly. Where trees are planted in rain gardens or widened verges, this effect is stronger still. The slowing may be modest around a single tree, but across a district thousands of small delays can change the peak of a flood. The result is not that floods disappear, but that pressure on storm drains is reduced at exactly the moments when the drainage network is most likely to fail.

¶4 The relationship between trees and air pollution is more complicated than many publicity campaigns suggest. Leaves and bark can trap fine particles from traffic, and some gases are absorbed through leaf surfaces. However, trees cannot remove pollution entirely, and badly placed planting may even trap dirty air at street level if it blocks ventilation in a narrow canyon of tall buildings. The lesson is not that trees are ineffective, but that their design matters. A line of trees chosen for beauty alone may do less good than a more carefully arranged mixture of species, heights and gaps that allows polluted air to disperse.

¶5 The benefits are not distributed evenly. In many cities, wealthier neighbourhoods have older, larger trees, while poorer districts have thinner canopies or none at all. This is partly a legacy of planning decisions, but it also reflects the fact that low-income streets often have less spare space and less political influence when budgets are allocated. Tree cover also affects how pleasant a street feels, which in turn influences whether people walk, linger, shop locally or allow children to play outside. The difference can reinforce existing inequalities. A child walking to school through a treeless estate may experience hotter air and dirtier traffic than a child in a leafier suburb, even though both live within the same city boundary.

¶6 Because the services trees provide are hard to see, urban foresters increasingly try to give them a financial value. They calculate the money saved when trees reduce energy use, delay the need for new drains or improve public health. Such figures are imperfect, but they help trees compete with other demands on city budgets. A mature tree that might once have been regarded as an obstacle to road works can instead be described as an asset that has taken decades to grow. Cutting it down, in that account, is not a minor tidying decision but the loss of a functioning piece of public infrastructure.

¶7 Still, trees are not a simple cure for urban problems. They need enough soil, regular care and room for roots and branches, all of which are difficult to provide in dense streets crowded with cables, pipes and parked cars. Young trees are especially vulnerable, and many die within a few years because they are planted in tiny pits and then neglected. Planting targets are therefore less important than survival rates. A city that plants thousands of saplings but fails to keep them alive has not created an urban forest; it has only staged a short-lived ceremony of good intentions.

questions.text: Urban trees are now described by some researchers as living infrastructure.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says researchers have begun to describe trees as living infrastructure.

evidence.quote: describe them as living infrastructure

questions.text: Trees cool cities only by providing shade.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says trees cool through both shade and evaporation, so shade is not the only mechanism.

evidence.quote: leaves release moisture through evaporation

questions.text: Urban trees can completely eliminate air pollution from traffic.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 states directly that trees cannot remove pollution entirely.

evidence.quote: trees cannot remove pollution entirely

questions.text: The passage says tree-lined streets always have lower crime rates than treeless streets.
questions.explanation: The passage discusses heat, water, pollution and inequality, but it makes no comparison about crime rates.

questions.text: Leaves cool the air by releasing moisture through ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says leaves release moisture through evaporation.

evidence.quote: through evaporation

questions.text: Districts of asphalt, brick and concrete that stay warmer are called heat ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 identifies warmer city districts as heat islands.

evidence.quote: cities suffer from heat islands

questions.text: Roots and loosened soil act like ______, holding water back after rain.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says roots and loosened soil act like sponges.

evidence.quote: act like sponges

questions.text: Which paragraph explains how the value of trees can be expressed in money?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says urban foresters increasingly try to give tree services a financial value.

evidence.quote: give them a financial value

questions.text: Which paragraph discusses unequal access to tree cover in rich and poor neighbourhoods?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 contrasts wealthier neighbourhoods with poorer districts that have thinner canopies or none.

evidence.quote: poorer districts have thinner canopies or none at all

questions.text: Which paragraph warns that planting many saplings is not enough if they do not survive?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says planting targets are less important than survival rates.

evidence.quote: Planting targets are therefore less important than survival rates

questions.text: According to Paragraph 4, badly placed trees may trap dirty air if they
options: block ventilation in a narrow street
correct_answer: block ventilation in a narrow street
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says badly placed planting may trap dirty air if it blocks ventilation in a narrow canyon of buildings.

evidence.quote: blocks ventilation in a narrow canyon

questions.text: What can trees reduce during sudden storms?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says trees reduce pressure on storm drains when the drainage network is likely to fail.

evidence.quote: pressure on storm drains is reduced

questions.text: Which TWO conditions does Paragraph 7 say urban trees need?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 lists enough soil, regular care and room for roots and branches as tree needs.

evidence.quote: They need enough soil, regular care and room

sections.title: Passage 2 - Science Done by the Crowd
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 14-26.
sections.passage:
Science Done by the Crowd

¶1 For most of the twentieth century, scientific research was imagined as the work of specialists inside universities, museums and government laboratories. Ordinary citizens might benefit from discoveries, or read simplified accounts of them, but they were rarely thought of as participants in the making of knowledge. That view is now changing. Across a growing number of fields, from astronomy to ecology, researchers are inviting volunteers to collect observations, classify data and monitor places that professional teams could never cover alone. The change has been encouraged not only by technology, but by pressure from communities that want research to address problems they actually experience. The practice is often called citizen science, although many researchers prefer community science when local people help shape the questions as well as gather the evidence.

¶2 The idea is not entirely new. Amateur naturalists recorded the arrival of migrating birds and the flowering of plants long before digital databases existed. Their notebooks, often kept for decades, are now valuable because they show how seasonal patterns have shifted. In some countries, these handwritten records are among the few sources that stretch back far enough to reveal the slow movement of spring. What is new is the scale and speed with which observations can be gathered. A birdwatcher can upload a sighting from a phone; a diver can report coral bleaching before leaving the beach; a person in a city apartment can help identify galaxies from telescope images displayed on a website. Millions of small acts, once scattered and private, can now become part of a common scientific record.

¶3 The greatest advantage of citizen science is coverage. Professionals cannot be everywhere, and many environmental changes are too local, too brief or too widely spread to be captured by a small research team. Volunteers can notice the first appearance of an invasive insect in a garden, measure rainfall in a remote village or count plastic waste along a coastline after a storm. They may also return to the same site week after week, building a continuity that short research grants rarely allow. When such reports are combined, they may reveal patterns invisible from any single location. In this respect, citizen science does not replace expert research; it extends the reach of expert research into places and times that experts would otherwise miss.

¶4 Quality, however, is the central concern. A volunteer may misidentify a species, use an instrument incorrectly or report an event twice. Successful projects therefore build checks into their design. Some ask several people to classify the same image and accept the result only when there is agreement. Others provide short training modules, photograph-based guides or automatic warnings when an observation falls outside the likely range. Increasingly, platforms also keep raw records, so that doubtful entries can be rechecked rather than silently discarded. The aim is not to pretend that volunteers are trained scientists. It is to create a system in which individual errors are expected, detected and reduced before the data are used.

¶5 Citizen science can also change the relationship between researchers and the public. In a project monitoring river pollution, for example, residents who collect water samples may become more confident in discussions with local authorities. They are no longer simply complaining about a dirty river; they are holding evidence in their hands. This can make research politically sensitive, because data gathered by citizens may challenge official claims or commercial interests. In places where official monitoring is rare, citizen data may be the first evidence that a problem exists at all. Yet that sensitivity is also part of its value. Knowledge created in public is harder to ignore than knowledge kept inside a technical report.

¶6 There are ethical risks. Volunteers may give many hours of unpaid labour to projects that advance academic careers, while receiving little recognition beyond a thank-you email. Communities may be asked to provide data about local problems without being involved in decisions about what happens next. In some cases, public location data can even expose rare species to collectors or identify vulnerable neighbourhoods in ways residents did not intend. These risks are sharper when projects involve indigenous lands, polluted housing or health information, where data can easily be separated from the people whose lives give it meaning. For this reason, serious citizen science now requires more than a clever app. It requires clear rules about credit, privacy, ownership and the right of participants to understand how their contributions will be used.

¶7 The future of the field will probably be mixed. Some projects will remain simple, with volunteers gathering observations designed entirely by professionals. Others will be more collaborative, beginning with the concerns of a community and ending with evidence that can support local action. Neither model is automatically superior; a global astronomy project and a neighbourhood air-quality campaign have different aims and need different forms of participation. The challenge is to match the form of participation to the question being asked, rather than treating volunteering as a cheap substitute for professional work. In practice, the best projects often combine professional methods with local persistence, producing data that are both technically credible and rooted in lived experience. What they share is a recognition that knowledge is not weakened when more people help to make it. Under the right conditions, it may become broader, more grounded and more trusted over time in many different settings and institutions.

sections.title: Passage 3 - Remembering in the Digital Age
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 27-40.
sections.passage:
Remembering in the Digital Age

¶1 The complaint that digital devices have damaged human memory is now familiar. We no longer remember phone numbers, routes across town or the details of appointments, because a device remembers them for us. To some critics, this seems like a clear decline: the mind has become lazy, outsourcing its work to a machine. Yet the history of memory suggests a more complicated story. Writing, printing, filing cabinets and photographs all changed what people chose to remember, and each was greeted by fears that the mind would weaken. Those fears were not entirely foolish, but neither did earlier tools destroy intelligence. They shifted the boundary between what had to be carried internally and what could be placed in a trusted external support. Every culture, in that sense, trains memory around the tools it provides and the habits those tools reward in daily life. The question is therefore not whether technology changes memory. It always has. The more useful question is what kinds of remembering are lost, and what kinds become possible.

¶2 Psychologists sometimes describe this as transactive memory: a system in which people remember not the information itself, but where to find it. Families, offices and research teams have long worked in this way. One person knows the password to the storage room, another remembers the client's history, and a third knows which archive contains the old contract. Search engines and cloud folders extend this arrangement beyond the group to the network. A person may not remember a fact, but may remember the phrase that will retrieve it, the website that is reliable or the folder where the document was stored. The skill lies partly in judging which source deserves confidence and which search result merely looks authoritative. In such cases, memory has not vanished; it has been reorganised around access.

¶3 This reorganisation brings advantages. Digital memory is vast, searchable and resistant to many forms of human forgetting. A photograph can preserve the look of a street that has been demolished; a message thread can recover the exact wording of an agreement; a shared calendar can coordinate a complex household more reliably than any one person's recollection. For historians, activists and ordinary families, such records may matter greatly. They can challenge false claims, preserve minority experiences and keep contact across distance. In societies where official archives have excluded many voices, personal digital records may become evidence that lives were lived and losses occurred. It would be foolish to dismiss these gains simply because they are stored outside the head.

¶4 But external memory has costs. When people know that information will be saved, they often pay less attention to it in the first place. Experiments suggest that the expectation of later access can reduce recall, even when people remember very well where the information is stored. There is also a difference between retrieving a fact and understanding it. A student who can instantly look up a formula may still fail to see how it connects to other ideas. The same problem appears when people quote articles they have barely read, mistaking retrieval for comprehension. Memory, in this deeper sense, is not a warehouse of facts but a structure of relationships. If too little is held internally, the mind has fewer materials with which to think.

¶5 Digital records can also produce too much remembering. Human forgetting is often useful: it softens embarrassment, allows grief to change shape and lets minor conflicts fade. Online, however, a careless remark, an old photograph or a teenage performance can remain searchable long after the person concerned has changed. The past becomes less a private layer of experience than a permanent public file. This can make identity feel fixed, as if every earlier version of the self must remain available for inspection. Employers, strangers and even friends may encounter an archive before they encounter the person who has outgrown it. The right to be forgotten is, in part, a response to the fear that perfect memory may be socially cruel.

¶6 A further problem is trust. Digital memories appear precise, but they are not neutral. Search results are ranked by commercial algorithms, photo collections are selected by platform design, and automatic reminders decide which anniversaries deserve attention. Even the apparent permanence of storage is fragile: formats become obsolete, companies close, passwords are lost and accounts are deleted. A life carefully documented on one platform can become inaccessible if the company changes its rules or if relatives cannot enter an account after a death. Dr Lina Ortega argues that the danger is not dependence on technology itself, but dependence without awareness. We need to know when we are remembering, when we are searching and when a system is quietly choosing what will be brought back to us.

¶7 The sensible response is not to abandon digital aids but to use them deliberately. Some information is worth entrusting to devices precisely because doing so frees attention for judgment, conversation and creative work. Other knowledge must be internalised because it forms the background against which new information can be understood. The skill of the digital age may be knowing the difference. Schools and workplaces therefore need to teach not only facts or tools, but the discipline of deciding what should be learned, what should be stored and what should be questioned. Without that discipline, convenience can quietly become dependence and abundance can become confusion. A strong memory today is not necessarily a mind crowded with details, nor a phone filled with reminders, but a practiced partnership between internal understanding and external record. In that partnership, technology is neither enemy nor saviour. It is a powerful but imperfect colleague, one that demands supervision and periodic doubt from its users.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says writing, printing, filing cabinets and photographs all changed what people chose to remember.

evidence.quote: all changed what people chose to remember

questions.text: Transactive memory involves remembering where information can be found.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 defines transactive memory as remembering where to find information rather than the information itself.

evidence.quote: remember not the information itself, but where to find it

questions.text: Digital records are always more reliable than human recollection.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 warns that digital memories are not neutral and that storage can be fragile.

evidence.quote: they are not neutral

questions.text: The passage says most people now take fewer photographs than they did ten years ago.
questions.explanation: Photographs are mentioned as records, but the passage gives no claim about whether people take fewer photographs now.

questions.text: Human forgetting can sometimes be useful.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says human forgetting is often useful.

evidence.quote: Human forgetting is often useful

options: the exact wording of an agreement

questions.text: The right to be forgotten is presented as a response to the fear that perfect memory may be
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 links the right to be forgotten to fear that perfect memory may be socially cruel.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says Ortega argues that the danger is dependence without awareness.

evidence.quote: dependence without awareness

questions.text: Search results are ranked by commercial ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says search results are ranked by commercial algorithms.

evidence.quote: ranked by commercial algorithms

questions.text: Families, offices and research teams have long used transactive memory.

evidence.quote: Families, offices and research teams have long worked in this way

questions.text: The mind needs internal material in order to think with it.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says if too little is held internally, the mind has fewer materials with which to think.

evidence.quote: the mind has fewer materials with which to think

questions.text: Which TWO statements reflect the writer's conclusion?
options: Some knowledge should be kept in devices
A good memory is only a crowded mind
correct_answer: Some knowledge should be kept in devices
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says some information is worth entrusting to devices and other knowledge must be internalised.
